text: In recent years, numerous chemical models have been built by astrophysicists to describe the physical processes which are occurring in the many components of the interstellar medium (ISM) and therefore to describe the ionic and molecular abundances of the gas and dust in these environments. The last few years have considerably improved the chemical predictions by providing a better chemical description due to the progress of laboratory experimental and theoretical methods in order to quantify and measure individual reactions close to the ISM conditions and to obtain the rate coefficients which quantify their reactivity. An additional important aspect, which is more and more associated to the ISM chemical modelling, is the dynamic description of the evolutions of the physical conditions of the ISM media, and specially for star forming regions. The purpose of this paper is to summarize the present status of the art in respect to the modelling of dark clouds, the first step in star and disk formation.
